old: Please turn off the light conveniently as you go.
new: Please turn off the lights as you leave.
reason: The translation is an awkward calque of 随手. 随手 means casually/without extra effort as you pass by, not 'conveniently'. The revised phrasing is more natural English.

old: Take an umbrella without extra trouble when you go out.
new: Grab an umbrella on your way out.
reason: The phrase 'without extra trouble' is an awkward calque. The natural English rendering of 随手 in this context is an incidental, casual action.

old: Bring me a cup of water without extra trouble.
new: Bring me a cup of water while you're at it.
reason: 'Without extra trouble' is an awkward calque. 'While you're at it' is the natural English equivalent of 随手 here.

old: Close the door without extra trouble.
new: Close the door on your way out.
reason: Same awkward calque issue as above.

old: This matter has nothing to do with another person.
new: This matter has nothing to do with anyone else.
reason: 'Another person' is unnatural here; 'anyone else' is the idiomatic English for 他人 in this context.

old: to discuss
new: to have a talk
reason: 谈话 means to have a (usually formal or serious) conversation/talk, not simply 'to discuss'. The sentences illustrate a manager summoning an employee for a talk, which is better captured by 'to have a talk'.

old: The manager sought him out to discuss.
new: The manager called him in for a talk.
reason: The original translation is incomplete and awkward. 经理找他谈话 means the manager summoned him for a formal conversation, not merely 'to discuss'.

old: He came to the interview wearing a suit ensemble.
new: He came to the interview wearing a suit.
reason: 'Suit ensemble' is redundant and unnatural. A 套西装 is simply 'a suit'.

old: Her paintings have a very distinguishing characteristic.
new: Her paintings have a very distinctive character.
reason: 'A very distinguishing characteristic' is awkward and redundant. 'Distinctive character' or 'distinctiveness' is natural English.

old: The cultural distinguishing characteristic of this city is very prominent.
new: The cultural distinctiveness of this city is very prominent.
reason: Same issue: 'cultural distinguishing characteristic' is awkward. 'Cultural distinctiveness' is more natural.

old: to question
new: to ask questions
reason: 提问 means to pose or ask questions (in a class, press conference, etc.), not 'to question' someone in the sense of interrogating or challenging. 'To ask questions' is more accurate.

old: The reporter questioned at the press conference.
new: The reporter asked questions at the press conference.
reason: Aligns with corrected definition; 'questioned at the press conference' is unnatural English.

old: Students can question the teacher at any time.
new: Students can ask the teacher questions at any time.
reason: 提问老师 means to ask the teacher a question, not to challenge or interrogate the teacher.

old: The way he questions is very direct.
new: The way he asks questions is very direct.
reason: Aligns with corrected definition.

old: 没有什么能替代家人的爱。
new: 他替我照看了孩子。
reason: The original sentence uses 替代, a different word from the headword 替. The headword 替 does not appear standalone. Replacing with a sentence that uses 替 correctly.

old: Nothing can replace the love of family.
new: He looked after my child in my place.
reason: Translation updated to match new sentence.

old: 这种材料可以替换旧材料。
new: 你去不了的话，我替你去。
reason: The original sentence uses 替换, a different compound word. Replacing with a sentence that uses standalone 替.

old: This material can replace the old one.
new: If you can't go, I'll go in your place.
reason: Translation updated to match new sentence.

old: tiāo
new: tiǎo
reason: 挑 meaning 'to incite/provoke' (as in 挑起) is read tiǎo (3rd tone), not tiāo (1st tone). The 1st tone tiāo covers 'to carry on a shoulder pole' and 'to choose'. Rows 4544–4545 correctly use tiāo for those meanings; 'to incite' requires tiǎo.

old: The mischievous him spilled water on the ground.
new: The mischievous boy spilled water on the ground.
reason: 'The mischievous him' is ungrammatical in English. The subject of the sentence is the child described throughout; 'the mischievous boy' is natural.

old: She gets up at seven as a regular routine.
new: She usually gets up at seven.
reason: 'As a regular routine' is wordy and awkward. 通常 is an adverb meaning 'usually/normally'; the simple adverb captures it better.

old: Under regular circumstances, he is not late.
new: Under normal circumstances, he is not late.
reason: 'Regular circumstances' is unnatural; 'normal circumstances' is the standard English phrase.

old: The statistics data illustrates the problem.
new: The statistical data illustrates the problem.
reason: 'Statistics data' is redundant and unnatural. 'Statistical data' is the correct English collocation.

old: Her grades in the class are very prominent.
new: Her grades in the class are outstanding.
reason: 'Prominent' is not natural for academic grades in English. 'Outstanding' is the natural collocation.

old: She wound the yarn into a ball-shaped object.
new: She wound the yarn into a ball.
reason: 'Ball-shaped object' is the literal gloss of 团 as a noun; 'a ball' is natural English.

old: 他每天上网络查资料。
new: 他每天上网查资料。
reason: '上网络' is unnatural Chinese; the standard expression is '上网'. 网络 is the noun 'Internet/network', but one says 上网 (go online), not 上网络.

old: He goes on the Internet every day to look up information.
new: He goes online every day to look up information.
reason: Translation updated to match corrected sentence; 'goes online' is more natural than 'goes on the Internet'.
